program_name: Bachelor of Music in Performance: Wind Instruments and Percussion

requirement_blocks.rules_and_restrictions: Includes one 400-level Theory elective (3 credits). Jazz 220 can be taken in place of Theory 239 and 249. Jazz 221 can be taken in place of Theory 240 and 250. (Jazz 220/221 may not be accepted as a prerequisite for some 400-level Theory courses.)
Includes one 400-level Musicology or Theory, or listed Jazz elective (3 credits). Select from Jazz 460, 467, 470, 479 for the 400-level elective.
Proficiency may waive course requirement for Piano 110, 111, and 112.
2 credits elected each term. Must include three terms of Small Ensemble (Ensemble 461, 463, 465, 466, 400, 475). Ensemble 460: Jazz Ensemble requires department approval.
Minimum 24 credits of Performance lessons required. Musperf 440: Senior Recital (0 credits) is required for completion. Typically 32 credits are taken over 8 terms (4 credits each).
Or equivalent.
3-4 credits. Course must be approved by Sweetland Writing Center.
3-4 credits. One course selected from approved courses within LSA (or within SMTD by petition). SMTD RE courses will not count into overall non-SMTD credits.

These credits are required to complete the minimum 30 credit hour non-music requirement (after First Year Writing, Upper Level Writing, and Race and Ethnicity). Courses within Arts Administration, Dance, and Theatre may also count as non-Music.
These credits are general electives to fulfill the overall 121 credit requirement. The sample schedule suggests 9 credits of general electives.

To declare a major in CE, students must have finished at least one full semester at U of M, have an overall GPA of 2.0 or better, and have credit (either by transfer or a letter grade) for at least one course in each of the following categories: Calculus (such as MATH 115, 116, 120, 121, 156); Calculus based physics lectures (such as PHYSICS 140 or 160) or chemistry lectures (CHEM 130); Engineering courses (such as ENGR 100 or [ENGR 101 or ENGR 151 or EECS 180 or EECS 183]).

At least 50 credits must be taken on the U of M Ann Arbor campus. At least 30 credits of technical coursework at the 300-level or higher must be completed on the U of M Ann Arbor Campus. All technical classes used toward CE requirements must be completed with a grade of a C or better. A grade of C- or below in any of the College Core, Program Core, or Technical Electives is considered a failing grade and the course must be repeated or substituted with another option. Grades of C- through D- are acceptable for Intellectual Breadth or General Electives.

No pass/fail allowed. Students must take either CHEM 125, CHEM 126, and CHEM 130 (5 credits total) OR CHEM 210 and CHEM 211 (4 credits total). Students must take ENGR 101 OR ENGR 151 OR EECS 180 (AP credit). Students must take MATH 115 OR MATH 120. Students must take MATH 116 OR MATH 121. Students must take one of MATH 214, MATH 215, MATH 217, MATH 417, or MATH 419. Students must take PHYSICS 140 and PHYSICS 141. Students must take PHYSICS 240 and PHYSICS 241.

Pass/fail allowed. At least 3 credits of Humanities classes marked HU in the LSA course guide are required. At least 3 credits must be at the 300 level or higher are required. Grades of C- through D- are acceptable.
Pass/fail allowed. Nearly any course at the University of Michigan can be taken as a General Elective. AP credits or transfer credits can be used here. Grades of C- through D- are acceptable.

No pass/fail allowed. Students must take EECS 301 OR MATH 425 OR STATS 412. Students must take EECS 496 OR ENGR 499-002. Capstone Course Structure Change (Beginning in Fall 2026): EECS 400 will replace EECS 496. During their MDE semester, CE students must enroll concurrently in EECS 400 (2 credits) or ENGR 499 (2 credits), TCHNCLCM 496 (2 credits), and an MDE Content Course (4 credits). If EECS 496 or ENGR 499 have already been completed, EECS 400 is not required.
Pass/fail allowed if student takes two other Core Electives and confirms via email. All technical classes must be completed with a grade of C or better.

Pass/fail allowed if student takes three other ULCEs and confirms via email. All technical classes must be completed with a grade of C or better. Must complete at least 10 credits from a minimum of 3 courses. One course must be a Major Design Experience course offering. No more than 4 credits of EECS 482 may be used toward ULCE Electives. Technical elective credits can 'flow down' from previous categories: once 10 credits are earned in Upper Level CE Electives, additional credits can flow down to EECS Electives. EECS Special Topics Classes can sometimes count toward Upper Level CE Electives.

All Computer Engineering students are required to complete at least one Major Design Experience class from this list. This is part of the Upper Level CE Electives requirement. Students can request special permission to use an MDE project course from another program, including the Multidisciplinary Design Program, but this class will generally not count as an Upper Level CE Elective; a CE petition is required for this option. EECS 470 satisfies the MDE requirement if completed in Fall 2025 or earlier. EECS 472 satisfies the MDE requirement if completed in Winter 2026 or later. By FA26, EECS 470 becomes a prerequisite, and EECS 472 becomes the required Major Design Experience Content Course.

Pass/fail allowed if student takes a different EECS Elective and confirms via email. All technical classes must be completed with a grade of C or better. Technical elective credits can 'flow down' from previous categories: once 3 credits are earned in EECS Electives, additional credits can flow down to Flexible Technical Electives. MECHENG 552 is only for dual CE-ME majors. Other EECS courses may be approved on a case-by-case basis. EECS Special Topics Classes can sometimes count toward EECS Electives.

Pass/fail allowed if student takes a different FTE and confirms via email. All technical classes must be completed with a grade of C or better. Other courses may be acceptable with prior approval of the Chief Program Advisor. A basic standard is that a class should involve at least as much technical content as the options on this list and involve the use of college-level mathematics. If little mathematics beyond arithmetic or basic algebra is involved, it is unlikely to be approved. All tutoring and seminar courses are excluded. Technical elective credits can 'flow down' from previous categories (Upper Level CE Electives, EECS Electives). *Directed Study Rule: Up to 4 credit hours of independent study (EECS 399, EECS 499, ENGR 355, ENGR 455 or other technical department's independent study courses) may be used as Flexible Technical Electives. ENGR 455 credits approved to fulfill the Major Design Experience requirement are exempt from this Directed Study Rule, meaning if ENGR 455 is used to satisfy your MDE checkbox, you can use 4 credits of ENGR 455 as well as up to 4 credits of Directed Study coursework toward your Flexible Technical Electives. EECS Special Topics Classes can sometimes count toward Flexible Technical Electives. For Aerospace Engineering, any 300-level & above except 495 (390 & 490 see Directed Study Rule). For Biology, any 400-level and above (see Directed Study Rule). For Biomedical Engineering, any 400-level and above (see Directed Study Rule). For Chemical Engineering, any 300-level and above (see Directed Study Rule). For Civil and Environmental Engineering, any 300-level and above (see Directed Study Rule). For Climate (Climate and Space Sciences Engineering), any 300-level or above (see Directed Study Rule). For Electrical Engineering and Computer Science, EECS 281 may only be used toward FTEs if other courses are used to fulfill the Core Electives area (EECS 281 cannot count in two spots). Any 300-level or above (except 314, 402, 403, 406, 409, 410 and 495) (see Directed Study Rule). For Industrial and Operations Engineering, IOE 202 is not open to students with senior standing. Any 300-level and above except 373 and 422 (see Directed Study Rule). For Materials Science and Engineering, any 300-level and above (see Directed Study Rule). For Mathematics, MATH 214, MATH 215, MATH 217 are only if not used toward CoE Common Core Requirements. Any class at the 300-level or higher (except 310, 327, 333, 385, 389, 399, 422, 429, 431, 485, 486, 489, 497). Students can only receive credit for one linear algebra course. For Mechanical Engineering, any 300-level and above (see Directed Study Rule). For Naval Architecture and Marine Engineering, any 300-level and above (see Directed Study Rule). For Nuclear Engineering and Radiological Sciences, any 300-level or above (see Directed Study Rule). For Physics, any 300-level and above (except 333, 334, 365, 420, 481; see Directed Study Rule). For Space (Climate and Space Sciences Engineering), any 300-level or above (see Directed Study Rule).

To declare a major in EE, students must have finished at least one full semester at U of M, have an overall GPA of 2.0 or better, and have credit (either by transfer or a letter grade) for at least one course in each of these categories: Calculus (such as MATH 115, 116, 120, 121, 156); Calculus based physics lectures (such as PHYSICS 140 or 160) or chemistry lectures (CHEM 130); Engineering courses (such as ENGR 100 or [ENGR 101 or ENGR 151 or EECS 180 or EECS 183]).

Must take all courses with a letter grade of a C or better. No pass/fail allowed. Course substitutions are allowed as follows: CHEM 125/126 (5 cr) or CHEM 210/211 (4 cr) for General Chemistry; ENGR 151 or EECS 180 (AP credit) for ENGR 101; MATH 120 for MATH 115; MATH 121 for MATH 116; PHYSICS 141 for PHYSICS 140; PHYSICS 241 for PHYSICS 240.
Pass/fail allowed. At least 3 credits of Humanities classes marked HU in the LSA course guide are required. At least 3 credits must be at the 300 level or higher are required.
Pass/fail allowed. Nearly any course at the University of Michigan can be taken as a General Elective.
Must take all courses with a letter grade of a C or better. No pass/fail allowed. ENGR 499-002 can substitute EECS 496.

Pass/fail allowed if student takes a different MDE and confirms via email. All Electrical Engineering students are required to complete at least one Major Design Experience class. Students may request special permission to use a senior design project course from another program, including ENGR 455, by submitting an EE petition. If approved, an additional 4 credits of Upper Level EE Electives are required for a total of 23 credits of Upper Level EE Electives. For 2025-2026, EECS 470 satisfies the MDE requirement if completed in Fall 2025 or earlier. EECS 472 satisfies the MDE requirement if completed in Winter 2026 or later. By Fall 2026, EECS 470 becomes a prerequisite, and EECS 472 becomes the required Major Design Experience Content Course. Starting in Fall 2026, EECS 400 (2 credits) will replace EECS 496 for all EE undergraduate students. During their MDE semester, EE students must enroll in EECS 400 (2 credits) [ENGR 499 (2 credits) also accepted], TCHNCLCM 496 (2 credits), and an MDE Content Course (4 credits) concurrently for a total of 8 credits. If EECS 496 or ENGR 499 have already been completed, EECS 400 is not required.

Pass/fail allowed if student takes a different ULEEE and confirms via email. Students are required to take 19 credits of Upper Level EE Electives from the pre-approved course list. At least 7 credits must be at the 400-level or higher. Courses must be chosen from at least two categories: Biomedical; Circuits & Solid State; Communication, Control & Signal Processing; Computers; Electromagnetics & Optics; Power. Courses with an asterisk (*) are MDE courses, where credit may be received as either an Upper Level EE Elective OR an MDE course. Courses with a dagger (†) may be used to satisfy the PES requirement. Physical Electronics and Semiconductor (PES) Requirement: Taking at least one course indicated with a dagger satisfies the PES requirement. The course satisfying this requirement may be counted toward Upper Level EE Electives or Flexible Technical Electives.

Pass/fail allowed if student takes a different FTE and confirms via email. Courses that allow students to customize their studies beyond the core EE curriculum, chosen from approved advanced courses in engineering, mathematics, the physical sciences, or other technically relevant fields. Up to 4 credit hours of independent study (EECS 399, EECS 499, ENGR 355, ENGR 455 or other technical department's independent study courses) may be used as Flexible Technical Electives. The ENGR 455 credits approved to fulfill the Major Design Experience requirement are exempt from this Directed Study Rule. For Mathematics courses: Credit will only be given for ONE of the following classes: MATH 214, 217, 417, 419 or 513. For EECS courses: Any 300-level or above (except 314, 402, 403, 406, 409, 410 and 495). For Robotics courses: ROB 101 is not currently approved to satisfy EE FTEs. For IOE courses: Any 300-level and above except 373 and 422. For Aerospace courses: Any 300-level+ except 495. For Biology courses: Any 400-level and above. For Biomedical Engineering courses: Any 400-level and above. For Chemical Engineering courses: Any 300-level and above. For Chemistry courses: Any 300-level or above. For Civil and Environmental Engineering courses: Any 300-level or above. For Climate (Climate and Space Sciences Engineering) courses: Any 300-level or above. For Materials Science and Engineering courses: Any 300-level and above. For Mechanical Engineering courses: Any 300-level and above. For Naval Architecture and Marine Engineering courses: Any 300-level and above. For Nuclear Engineering and Radiological Sciences courses: Any 300-level and above. For Physics courses: Any 300-level and above (except 333, 334, and 420). EE majors pursuing the CS minor may only double count one class, EECS 280, between their major and minor requirements. Additional classes used to satisfy CS minor requirements, such as EECS 203, EECS 281 and the additional CS elective, will only count toward General Electives in Electrical Engineering.

To declare a major in CE, you'll need: 1. To have finished at least one full semester at U of M. 2. An overall GPA of 2.0 or better. 3. To have credit (either by transfer or a letter grade) for at least one course in each of these categories: a. Calculus (such as MATH 115, MATH 116, MATH 120, MATH 121, MATH 156). b. Calculus based physics lectures (such as PHYSICS 140 or PHYSICS 160) or chemistry lectures (CHEM 130). c. Engineering courses (such as ENGR 100 or (ENGR 101 or ENGR 151 or EECS 180 or EECS 183)).

Students must complete one of the following chemistry sequences: (CHEM 125, CHEM 126, and CHEM 130) or (CHEM 210 and CHEM 211). Students must complete one of the following: ENGR 101, ENGR 151, EECS 180, or EECS 183. Students must complete one of the following: MATH 115 or MATH 120. Students must complete one of the following: MATH 116 or MATH 121. Students must complete one of the following: MATH 214, MATH 215, MATH 217, MATH 417, or MATH 419.
At least 3 credits of Humanities classes marked HU in the LSA course guide are required. At least 3 credits must be at the 300 level or higher are required.

Nearly any course at the University of Michigan can be taken as a General Elective and this is how you get to a total of 128 credits. You might find that you have AP credits or transfer credits that can be used here.
Students must choose one from EECS 301, MATH 425, or STATS 412. Students must choose one from EECS 496 or ENGR 499-002.
Computer Engineering students must complete 8 credits (2 courses) from the listed options.

No more than 4 credits of EECS 482 may be used toward ULCE Electives. Technical elective credits can 'flow down' from previous categories. EECS 472 satisfies the MDE requirement if completed in Winter 2026 or later. Note that some courses are not offered every semester.

Other EECS courses may be approved on a case-by-case basis. Technical elective credits can 'flow down' from previous categories. Once 3 credits in EECS Electives are earned, additional credits can flow down to Flexible Technical Electives.

A rough guideline is that it should involve the use of college-level mathematics. If little mathematics beyond arithmetic or basic algebra is involved, it is unlikely to be approved. All tutoring and seminar courses are excluded. Students can only receive credit for one linear algebra course. For EECS: Any 300-level or above (except EECS 314, EECS 402, EECS 403, EECS 406, EECS 409, EECS 410 and EECS 495). EECS 281 may only be used toward FTEs if other courses are used to fulfill the Core Electives area - EECS 281 cannot count in two spots. Students must complete either (CHEM 130 and (CHEM 125 or CHEM 126)) OR (CHEM 210 and CHEM 211). 16 credits of Intellectual Breadth are required, which must include either ECON 101 or ECON 102. A grade of C- or better is required for courses in this category.
A grade of C- or better is required for courses in this category.

Choose one course from the listed options. A grade of C- or better is required for courses in this category.

At least five of the seven program electives are required. CEE may accept equivalent courses offered by other departments in the College of Engineering, with permission from the program advisor. A grade of C- or better is required for courses in this category.

A sixth program elective may count as a technical elective. At least one of the three technical electives must be a design course (design courses are marked with an *). CEE may accept study abroad courses as a technical elective with prior approval of the department. A grade of C- or better is required for courses in this category.

If you have a satisfactory score or grade in Chemistry AP, A-Level, IB Exams, or transfer credit from another institution for CHEM 130/125/126, you will have met the Chemistry Core Requirement. Note 2: If you have a satisfactory score or grade in Physics AP, A-Level, IB Exams, or transfer credit from another institution for PHYSICS 140/141 and 240/241, you will have met the Physics Core.

college: College of Literature, Science, and the Arts (LSA)

requirement_blocks.rules_and_restrictions: Students normally declare a major during the second term of their sophomore year by contacting the appropriate department and meeting with an advisor. Requirements are based on the time of declaration. Majors and minors are listed on the official transcript upon graduation, but not on the diploma unless receiving Honors. Honors students may propose an Individualized Major Program.

Course requirements range from 24 to 48 credits at the 200-level and above. No more than 60 credits in a major (including one department and required cognates) may count toward the 120 degree credits. For departments with multiple SUBJECTs, the 60-credit limit applies to the whole department. No more than 60 credits in one non-English language may count, unless excess credits meet the language requirement. Area, interdepartmental, or special majors may count no more than 60 credits in any one department.

Major courses and required cognates cannot be taken Pass/Fail. A department may allow Credit/No Credit for specific courses, but all others must be taken for a grade. An overall GPA of at least 2.0 is required in all courses taken in the major (including prerequisites, electives, and cognates). Honors majors require an overall GPA of 3.4 or higher, depending on the department.

A maximum of one course may be shared between a major and a minor. No course may be shared between a major and a supplemental studies program. Courses from the major department cannot count toward distribution plans unless the student is pursuing multiple majors (courses from one major can fulfill distribution). Major courses may also meet the Upper-Level Writing Requirement, Race & Ethnicity Requirement, or Quantitative Reasoning Requirement.
Test credits may not be used to meet the requirements of a major, but they may be used to meet prerequisites (unless a departmental exception is granted by the LSA Curriculum Committee).

program_name: LSA Multiple Majors, Honors, and Additional Policies
college: College of Literature, Science, and the Arts (LSA)

requirement_blocks.rules_and_restrictions: Students must meet all requirements for both majors. Courses, including cognates, from one major may be used to satisfy requirements of the second major when appropriate. Courses from one major may be used toward distribution. Plans must be developed and approved by a department advisor.

Students may complete a second major after graduation by registering as a non-degree candidate through Admissions. An advisor must submit a release form upon completion. Students cannot add, complete, or declare a minor after graduation, but minor credits can be used toward an additional major (the minor is replaced by the major on the transcript). Degree types (e.g., A.B. to B.S.) cannot be changed after graduation.

Students typically apply at the beginning of their third year. Requires a cumulative GPA of 3.4 to graduate with Honors (some departments require higher). Must complete at least 58 credits in residence. Requires completion of a Senior Honors thesis involving original research under a faculty mentor (with few exceptions like Math and CS), and sometimes comprehensive exams. Thesis work may receive a 'Y' (work in progress) grade until completion. Departments recommend 'No Honors' (if < 3.4 GPA or standards not met), 'Honors', 'High Honors', or 'Highest Honors'. Exceptions to the GPA minimum can be petitioned with compelling evidence.

Intended for exceptional students pursuing an interdepartmental/interdisciplinary liberal arts program not currently available. Must have an identifiable academic focus within LSA, disciplinary base, unifying theme, and culminate in an Honors thesis. Requires an overall GPA of 3.5, and typically a 3.7 in HIMP-related courses. Students must submit a formal prospectus to the Honors Academic Board. Contact the Associate Director of the Honors Program to begin.

requirement_blocks.block_name: Honors Thesis Additional Policies (Grants, ULWR, Archives)
requirement_blocks.rules_and_restrictions: Seniors are eligible for thesis/travel grants (Graf, Hellman, Davidson, Kennedy, Cutcheon). The Senior Thesis can be used to satisfy the Upper-Level Writing Requirement (ULWR) by submitting a Sweetland Center for Writing Thesis Intent Form early in the finishing term. Graduates are encouraged to deposit their completed thesis in the Deep Blue electronic archive.

college: College of Literature, Science, and the Arts (LSA) and Participating Units

requirement_blocks.rules_and_restrictions: Electing a minor is optional, and there is no limit to the number of minors a student may elect. Must require at least 15 credits, show structure and coherence, and contain upper-level courses. At least 9 out of the 15 credits must be taken in residence. Noted on transcript, not diploma. Plan must be developed and approved with an advisor. Not available to B.G.S. degree students. Individually designed minors are not allowed.

Students must earn an overall GPA of at least 2.0 in the minor, including necessary prerequisites. Pass/fail grading is not permitted for minor courses. Departments may allow credit/no credit for specific courses, but all others must be taken for a grade.

No course may be shared between multiple minors or between a minor and a supplemental studies program. A maximum of one course may be shared between a minor and a major (with multiple majors, the shared course can be different for each major or the same for multiple majors). Minor prerequisites can overlap with major prerequisites. Minor courses can also meet area distribution plans, Language, Upper-Level Writing, Race and Ethnicity, or Quantitative Reasoning requirements.

Test credits may be used to meet prerequisites, but not the requirements of the minor itself. Minors cannot be added, completed, or declared after graduation. However, minor credits can be used toward an additional major post-graduation (the minor is then replaced by the major on the transcript).

Students in other U-M units (Architecture & Urban Planning, Art & Design, Business, Dental Hygiene, Education, Engineering, Information, Kinesiology, Music, Theatre & Dance, Natural Resources, Nursing, Pharmacy, Public Health, Public Policy) may pursue LSA minors. Limitations may apply; students must consult their home unit advisor.

requirement_blocks.block_name: Minors Offered by Other Academic Units
requirement_blocks.rules_and_restrictions: Other units offering minors include: Art & Design (Art and Design); Ross (Business); Education (Education for Empowerment); Engineering (Multidisciplinary design, Electrical engineering, Computer science, Climate and space sciences. *Note: International minor and Environmental engineering are not open to LSA students*); Kinesiology (Movement science - *open only to SMTD dance majors*); SMTD (Music, Global theatre and ethnic studies, Performing arts management, Design and production); Social Work (Community Action and Social Change); Medical School (Human Anatomy and Physiology).

college: University of Michigan - Department of Psychology

requirement_blocks.rules_and_restrictions: Students may declare the Psychology major once they have passed an Introductory Psychology course (with a 'C' or better), Statistics 206/250/280 (with a 'C-' or better, or Pass/P), and completed the online Pre-Major Tutorials at https://lsa.umich.edu/psych/undergraduates/major-in-psychology-or-bcn/how-to-declare. Credits from pre-requisites do not count toward the 32 required major credits.
Complete one Introductory Psychology Course (with minimum grade of 'C').
Complete one Statistics Course (with a minimum grade of 'C-' or Pass/P).
Complete three Breadth Group Courses: One course from each of the following three breadth groups must be completed. Additional breadth courses do not count towards the major credits.

The lab requirement must be met in one of the following three ways for a minimum of 6 credits. Each course must be taken for at least 3 credits in a single term. Option 1: One course from Methods-Based Labs (A) AND one course from Experiential Labs (B). Option 2: Two courses from Methods-Based Labs (A). Option 3: Honors Thesis or Senior Thesis sequence. Courses that are graded 'credit/no credit' may be used to satisfy the categorical requirement for a laboratory course, but do not count toward the 32 credits needed to complete the Psychology major.
*Experiential based labs that are graded Credit/No Credit may fulfill the categorical requirement for this lab; however, the credits do not count towards the 32 major credits.

The Senior and Honors thesis sequences are advanced research opportunities during the final two semesters. To be eligible, students must gain significant research experience prior to their final year. Psych 424 and 428 fulfill the experiential/research lab; Psych 426 and 429 fulfill the methods lab and ULWR. Interested students should consult with an advisor and view the website for more information: https://lsa.umich.edu/psych/undergraduates/honors-program/for-prospective-honors-students.html

requirement_blocks.block_name: Major Requirements: Upper Level Elective Requirement
requirement_blocks.rules_and_restrictions: At least 4 courses for at least 12 credits: 2 courses must be at the 300-level and at least 1 course at the 400-level. 1 off-tier course may be substituted for a 300-level course (see below). Psych 420/421 may NOT be the only 400-level course. For a complete list of courses that count towards this requirement, view the term-specific course charts: https://lsa.umich.edu/psych/undergraduates/major-in-psychology-or-bcn/course-info.html
One course from this list may be substituted for a 300-level upper level elective course.

A minimum of 32 credits are required. The pre-requisite courses are not included in the total credits. If you have completed all the above coursework but have not reached 32 credits, you must complete additional Psych coursework at the 300-level or above until at least 32 credits are achieved.

requirement_blocks.block_name: General Program Rules and GPA Requirement
requirement_blocks.rules_and_restrictions: Must earn a cumulative grade point average (GPA) of at least 2.0 in ALL courses taken within the Psychology Department, as well as any non-Psych courses that are counting towards the major. Stats is not included in the major GPA. Transfer courses are not calculated into the GPA unless the grade appears on the official UM transcript. Once you have declared, your major GPA can be viewed through your LSA Audit Checklist: https://myadvising.lsa.umich.edu/audit-checklist. All courses, with the exception of the Stats pre-requisite and non-graded labs, must be taken for a letter grade. Psychology majors are urged to consult with an advisor within the Department at least once per year to review their progress toward the major requirements. When taking a course that is crosslisted under multiple departments (i.e. PSYCH 240 / NEURO 240), you can register under EITHER department. It will count towards your major the same way, regardless. Transfer courses may be applied toward your major, but at least 24 credits must be completed in-residence through the University of Michigan's Department of Psychology (includes pre-requisite and major credits). Of those 24 credits, at least 12 credits must be in upper level courses (300+, letter graded) in Psychology. Visit https://lsa.umich.edu/psych/undergraduates/transfer-credit for more information.

college: University of Michigan - Department of Psychology

Credits from pre-requisites do not count toward the 32 required major credits.

college: College of Literature, Science, and the Arts

requirement_blocks.rules_and_restrictions: Must be taken before EECS 280. Must have a final letter grade of C or higher.

Academic Good Standing is required for declaration.

15 credits must be in approved Upper Level CS (ULCS) Electives. At least 12 credits must be from the ULCS list, while the remaining 3 credits can be from the Expanded ULCS list. An EECS course may only count toward one requirement—either ULCS or Capstone, not both. All ULCS are included in the Expanded ULCS list. Students must be declared CS-LSA before they can register for ULCS courses. ULCS elective credit is rarely transferable from an outside institution. Some special topics courses are designated as ULCS in certain terms. Must have a final letter grade of C or higher.

Students must take an approved EECS CS Capstone course to fulfill the Capstone requirement. Honors thesis (EECS 443) is an option. An EECS course may only count toward one requirement—either ULCS or Capstone, not both. Must have a final letter grade of C or higher.

To declare a major in CS-Eng, you must be a College of Engineering student and: 1. Students admitted to the University of Michigan Fall 2023 or later: must be selected to study CS through one of the approved selection pathways in order to declare CS-Eng. 2. Have completed at least one full term or 12 total credits at UM Ann Arbor. 3. Have an overall UM GPA of 2.0 or better in courses taken at the UM Ann Arbor campus and be in good standing. Grades of 'C' or better must be achieved in all courses used to satisfy the pre-declaration and other major requirements.
Must complete one of these courses with a grade of C or better.

requirement_blocks.block_name: College of Engineering Core Requirements - Engineering Introductory Courses
requirement_blocks.rules_and_restrictions: Must take ENGR 100 and one course from the elective options.

requirement_blocks.block_name: College of Engineering Core Requirements - Chemistry
requirement_blocks.rules_and_restrictions: Must take CHEM 125, CHEM 126, and CHEM 130 OR CHEM 210 and CHEM 211.

requirement_blocks.block_name: College of Engineering Core Requirements - Physics
College of Engineering Core Requirements - Mathematics
requirement_blocks.rules_and_restrictions: Must satisfy requirements for MATH 115 (or alternatives), MATH 116 (or alternatives), MATH 214 (or alternatives), and MATH 215 or MATH 216 (or alternatives). If both MATH 215 and MATH 216 are taken, MATH 216 can count as a Flexible Technical Elective.

requirement_blocks.block_name: College of Engineering Core Requirements - Intellectual Breadth
requirement_blocks.rules_and_restrictions: Rules for this College requirement can be found in the CoE Bulletin under the 'Intellectual Breadth' heading.

requirement_blocks.block_name: College of Engineering Core Requirements - General Electives
requirement_blocks.rules_and_restrictions: CoE degrees require 128 total credits, and more or fewer GE credits may be needed to achieve this total depending on individual factors in a student's record.

Must take EECS 203 (or MATH 465/565), EECS 280, EECS 281, EECS 370, EECS 376, and EECS 496. Must take one Probability and Statistics course from the options. Must take TCHNCLCM 300. *Note that MATH 465/565 requires significantly more mathematical background than does EECS 203. Speak to an advisor before selecting these courses. IOE 265 is generally open only to undeclared or IOE students. Students with credit from Statistics AP exams should pursue STATS 280 for this requirement. For students taking ROBOTICS 204 as a Flexible Technical Elective, the TCHNCLCM 300 requirement will be waived. EECS 496 can be satisfied by ENGR 499-002, COMPFOR 111, CSE 543, COMM 349, or approved Special Topics sections. Students are limited to attempting each of the three 200-level courses (EECS 203, EECS 280, EECS 281) at most twice.
A minimum of 25 additional credits of technical electives is required.

requirement_blocks.block_name: CS-Eng Program Requirements - Upper-Level CS Technical Electives
requirement_blocks.rules_and_restrictions: At least 12 credits must be from the ULCS list, while the remaining 3 credits can be from the Expanded ULCS list. All ULCS are included in the Expanded ULCS list.

requirement_blocks.block_name: CS-Eng Program Requirements - Flexible Technical Electives
requirement_blocks.rules_and_restrictions: The remainder of the technical elective credits may be chosen from the approved Flexible Technical Electives lists. A maximum of 4 credits of EECS 499/399 (or other upper-level directed/independent study) may count in Flexible Technical Electives; additional credits will count as general electives.

requirement_blocks.block_name: CS-Eng Program Requirements - Major Design Experience
requirement_blocks.rules_and_restrictions: Must take an approved CS MDE course (4 credits). TCHNCLCM 497 (2 credits) must be taken in the same or later semester as the MDE (preferably the same semester). The MDE is a capstone design project that is recommended to be taken during one of your final two semesters. See page 5 for the current list of MDE courses.

To declare a major in CS-Eng, you must be a College of Engineering student and: 1. Students admitted to the University of Michigan Fall 2023 or later: must be selected to study CS through one of the approved selection pathways in order to declare CS-Eng. 2. Have completed at least one full term or 12 total credits at UM Ann Arbor. 3. Have an overall UM GPA of 2.0 or better in courses taken at the UM Ann Arbor campus and be in good standing. Grades of 'C' or better must be achieved in all courses used to satisfy the pre-declaration and other major requirements.

62 Ross Credits, 54 U-M Credits, and 4 Ross/U-M Elective Credits are required for a total of 120 credits.
Includes Signature Learning Experiences. Transfer students may take BA 102 (1 credit) instead of BA 100.
Students must complete all fixed and floating core courses by the end of the Senior Fall semester.
Students must take at least one capstone course.
16-18 credits or 6-7 classes, including approved Global Student Experiences coursework, must be completed by graduation.
These credits fulfill the remaining Ross credit requirement.
First-year writing requirement; Calculus I, II, or III.

Complete 3 of 4 distribution categories by graduation: Foreign Language (4th semester proficiency. Last semester must be taken for a grade); Humanities (9 credits); Social Science (9 credits, not including ECON 101 or 102); Natural Science or MSA (9 credits).
These credits fulfill the remaining U-M credit requirement.
Four additional Ross or U-M credits must be completed before graduation.

Milestone 1, 2, 3, and 4. The Inclusive Leaders Pathway offers a structured developmental journey grounded in self-reflection, personal growth, and leadership that places people and relationships at its core. At Ross, you can expect to build essential competencies to work across cultural and organizational contexts and lead with empathy, clarity, and purpose.

Students must satisfy the prerequisite. Options include: one of the following pairs (MATH 115 and MATH 116; MATH 185 and MATH 186; MATH 275 and MATH 276; or MATH 295 and MATH 296); or MATH 156; or Advanced Placement credits for MATH 120 and MATH 121. All courses must be completed with a grade of C- or better.

Students must elect 1 to 3 courses from this block. Only one course may be elected from each of the three areas (Multivariable Calculus, Linear Algebra, Differential Equations). All courses must be completed with a grade of C- or better.

A student may elect between 2 and 4 upper-level courses. The upper-level courses are not restricted, so a student may elect multiple courses from the same area. All courses must be completed with a grade of C- or better. A student must select at least 5 courses in total for the minor, including at least 1 basic course and at least 2 upper-level courses. Per LSA rule, students must take at least 9 credits in-residence. All in-residence credits must be taken from the Mathematics Department. In all cases, more advanced courses may be substituted with the approval of a math advisor. Classes offered outside of Mathematics cannot be used to satisfy the requirements of the minor.
